From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Bruneian culture](/w/index.php?title=Bruneian_culture&redirect=no "Bruneian culture"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/2023_Acara_Pentas_Perayaan_%28Tutong%29_15.jpg/250px-2023_Acara_Pentas_Perayaan_%28Tutong%29_15.jpg)](/wiki/File:2023_Acara_Pentas_Perayaan_(Tutong)_15.jpg)

Residents of [Tutong](/wiki/Tutong_District "Tutong District") during a performance in 2023.

The **culture of [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei")** is strongly influenced by [Malay](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)") culture and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). The culture is also influenced by the demographic makeup of the country: more than two-thirds of the population are [Malay](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), and the remainder consists of [Ethnic Chinese in Brunei](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_Brunei "Ethnic Chinese in Brunei"), [Indians](/wiki/Indians_in_Brunei "Indians in Brunei") and indigenous groups such as [Muruts](/wiki/Murut_people "Murut people"), [Bisaya Brunei](/wiki/Bisaya_(Borneo) "Bisaya (Borneo)"), [Brunei Dusun](/wiki/Brunei_Dusun "Brunei Dusun") and [Kedayans](/wiki/Kedayan "Kedayan").[[1]](#cite_note-ciafactbook-1) While Standard Bahasa Melayu is the official language of Brunei, languages such as [Bahasa Brunei](/w/index.php?title=Bahasa_Brunei&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bahasa Brunei (page does not exist)") and [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") are more commonly spoken.

Islam is the official religion of Brunei and Brunei has implemented [Sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") since 2014.

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Language")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Bolkiah_Garrison_19_February_2023.jpg/250px-Bolkiah_Garrison_19_February_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bolkiah_Garrison_19_February_2023.jpg)

A sign with both English and Malay instructions.

Main article: [Languages of Brunei](/wiki/Languages_of_Brunei "Languages of Brunei")

[Standard Malay](/wiki/Standard_Malay "Standard Malay"), the language spoken by the ethnic [Malays](/wiki/Malay_people "Malay people"), who make up over 65% of Brunei's population,[[1]](#cite_note-ciafactbook-1) is the official language of Brunei.[[2]](#cite_note-bilingualencyclopedia-2) However, the most widely spoken language in Brunei is [Brunei Malay](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay"), which differs greatly from Standard Malay in areas like [pronunciation](/wiki/Pronunciation "Pronunciation"), [lexis](/wiki/Lexis_(linguistics) "Lexis (linguistics)") and [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax").[[3]](#cite_note-bruneienglish-3) In addition, there are many other [Malay dialects](/wiki/Malayan_languages "Malayan languages") spoken by [indigenous communities](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples"),[[3]](#cite_note-bruneienglish-3) such as [Dayak](/wiki/Dayak_people#Languages "Dayak people") and [Iban](/wiki/Iban_language "Iban language").[[2]](#cite_note-bilingualencyclopedia-2) Usage of such Malay dialects has been on the decline due to assimilation of such ethnic culture with the mainstream culture; some varieties of these dialects are at risk of [becoming extinct](/wiki/Extinct_language "Extinct language").[[3]](#cite_note-bruneienglish-3)

[English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") is also used in Brunei.[[2]](#cite_note-bilingualencyclopedia-2) It is taught at both primary and secondary levels in Brunei schools and frequently seen in street signage throughout the country.[[2]](#cite_note-bilingualencyclopedia-2) [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), the language used by the sizable minority Chinese community, is widely used as well.[[4]](#cite_note-minoritylanguage-4) Although not officially recognized, the language is taught at certain Chinese-run schools and as an elective subject at the state-run [Universiti Brunei Darussalam](/wiki/Universiti_Brunei_Darussalam "Universiti Brunei Darussalam").[[4]](#cite_note-minoritylanguage-4) Chinese dialects such as [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") are commonly spoken in the Chinese community,[[4]](#cite_note-minoritylanguage-4) although their usage has been declining as the younger generation pick up Mandarin Chinese at the expense of dialects.[[3]](#cite_note-bruneienglish-3)

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Brunei_16_July_2023_64.jpg/250px-Brunei_16_July_2023_64.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brunei_16_July_2023_64.jpg)

[Kampong Telisai Mosque](/wiki/Kampong_Telisai "Kampong Telisai")

Main article: [Religion in Brunei](/wiki/Religion_in_Brunei "Religion in Brunei")

See also: [Islam in Brunei](/wiki/Islam_in_Brunei "Islam in Brunei"), [Christianity in Brunei](/wiki/Christianity_in_Brunei "Christianity in Brunei"), and [Freedom of religion in Brunei](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Brunei "Freedom of religion in Brunei")

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the official religion of Brunei; Muslims make up over 78% of the population.[[1]](#cite_note-ciafactbook-1) The [Constitution of Brunei](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brunei "Constitution of Brunei") states that "The religion of Brunei Darussalam shall be the Muslim religion according to the [Shafi'i](/wiki/Shafi%27i "Shafi'i") sect of that religion", although it also guarantees religious freedom, albeit with restrictions.[[5]](#cite_note-statedepartment-5) The ruling [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") is depicted as the defender of Islam in the country under a system known as Malay
Islamic Monarchy,[[6]](#cite_note-bbc-6) while the state's Ministry of Religious Affairs officially promotes Islam in Brunei.[[7]](#cite_note-michiganstate-7)

In 2014, Brunei controversially adopted [Sharia law](/wiki/Sharia_law "Sharia law"), becoming the first South-East Asian country to do so.[[6]](#cite_note-bbc-6) These laws apply to non-Muslims in the country and have been criticized by international organisations such as the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International").[[8]](#cite_note-cnn20140501-8) Prior to the introduction of Sharia law, Brunei had already adopted religiously-motivated laws such as the prohibition of the sale of alcohol in the country.[[8]](#cite_note-cnn20140501-8)

[Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") and [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") make up the largest proportion of non-Muslims in Brunei.[[1]](#cite_note-ciafactbook-1) There are several Christian churches, Chinese Buddhist or [Daoist](/wiki/Daoist "Daoist") temples and [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") temple, but these non-Muslim places of worship are not allowed to display visible signs of their religion outside of their premises.[[7]](#cite_note-michiganstate-7) These religions are not permitted to [proselytize](/wiki/Proselytize "Proselytize") or to try and convert others into their faith.[[7]](#cite_note-michiganstate-7) A non-Muslim woman marrying a Muslim is required by law to convert into Islam.[[7]](#cite_note-michiganstate-7)

## Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

**Bruneian cuisine** concerns the cuisine of [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"). It is similar to, and heavily influenced by the cuisine of neighbouring Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia, with additional influences from India, China, Thailand, and Middle East.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As is common in the region, fish and rice are staple foods, though beef is expensive and thus less common. Due to the predominance of the Islamic religion, the food is [halal](/wiki/Halal "Halal") and pork is avoided. Alcohol is banned in Brunei. In rural areas, game animals such as wild birds, [sambar deer](/wiki/Sambar_deer "Sambar deer"), and [barking deer](/wiki/Barking_deer "Barking deer") are hunted.

## Foods and dishes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foods and dishes")]

Dishes from Brunei are often [spicy](/wiki/Piquance "Piquance") and are commonly eaten with either [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice") or [noodles](/wiki/Noodle "Noodle"). [Nasi katok](/wiki/Nasi_katok "Nasi katok"), [beef rendang](/wiki/Beef_rendang "Beef rendang"), [nasi lemak](/wiki/Nasi_lemak "Nasi lemak"), and pajeri nanas are popular foods in Brunei. Among the few dishes peculiar to Brunei is *[ambuyat](/wiki/Ambuyat "Ambuyat")*, a sticky ball of flavourless [sago](/wiki/Sago "Sago") starch, which is wrapped around a bamboo fork and dipped into a spicy and sour gravy. *Nasi katok*, which literally means 'knock rice', consists of plain rice, fried chicken and sambal, a spicy relish made from ground chili peppers and a variety of secondary ingredients, including shrimp paste, garlic, ginger, shallot, scallion, palm sugar, lime juice, vinegar, and anchovies. Traditionally, *nasi katok* is served and wrapped in brown paper.

## Beverages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Beverages")]

Common drinks include [coconut milk](/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk"), [fruit juice](/wiki/Fruit_juice "Fruit juice"), [tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea"), [milk tea](/wiki/Milk_tea "Milk tea") and [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee").

Main article: [Cuisine of Brunei](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Brunei "Cuisine of Brunei")

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Jerudong-Lun_Bawang_27_May_2023_66.jpg/250px-Jerudong-Lun_Bawang_27_May_2023_66.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerudong-Lun_Bawang_27_May_2023_66.jpg)

A football match being played during the [2023 Brunei Super League](/wiki/2023_Brunei_Super_League "2023 Brunei Super League").

Main article: [Sports in Brunei](/wiki/Sports_in_Brunei "Sports in Brunei")

See also: [Football in Brunei](/wiki/Football_in_Brunei "Football in Brunei") and [Brunei at the Olympics](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Olympics "Brunei at the Olympics")

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is an extremely popular sport in Brunei.[[9]](#cite_note-bt20100602-9)

**Sport in Brunei** covers the variety of [sports](/wiki/Sport "Sport") that are played, from amateur to professional levels, across the [sultanate](/wiki/Sultanate "Sultanate") of [Brunei Darussalam](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), in addition to individuals and teams who are sent to compete in tournaments both home and abroad. [Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport played in Brunei. Several sports, along with leisure activities are also partaken in Brunei; from [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") and [swimming](/wiki/Swimming "Swimming") to [horseback riding](/wiki/Equestrianism "Equestrianism"), [mountain biking](/wiki/Mountain_biking "Mountain biking"), and [paintball](/wiki/Paintball "Paintball").

## Individual sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Individual sports")]

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sports")]

See also: [Outdoor recreation](/wiki/Outdoor_recreation "Outdoor recreation")

Recreational activities available include playing [Valorant](/wiki/Valorant "Valorant") on an international stage. Some tournaments like premier has been played in here and the Brunei team [Clown 9](/w/index.php?title=Clown_9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clown 9 (page does not exist)") won and crowned themselves as national champions with a exceptional performance of Reddd and JD03BS

Around 4,500 [hectares](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") (11,000 [acres](/wiki/Acre "Acre")) of undeveloped [coral reef](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") can be found in the waters off the coast of Brunei. Sunken [oil rigs](/wiki/Oil_rig "Oil rig") and [shipwrecks](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck") can be found far below the surface of the water. The [underwater diving](/wiki/Underwater_diving "Underwater diving") scene has attracted increasing numbers of local and foreign divers.

### Racquet sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Racquet sports")]

See also: [Racquet sport](/wiki/Racquet_sport "Racquet sport")

The [Brunei national badminton team](/wiki/Brunei_national_badminton_team "Brunei national badminton team") represents Brunei in international [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") competitions. In [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), Brunei first participated in the [1986 Thomas & Uber Cup](/wiki/1986_Thomas_%26_Uber_Cup "1986 Thomas & Uber Cup") qualification rounds. The Bruneian men's team participated in the [1999 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/1999_Southeast_Asian_Games "1999 Southeast Asian Games") for the first time as the host nation. In the quarter-finals of the [2001 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2001_Southeast_Asian_Games "2001 Southeast Asian Games"), Brunei lost to Malaysia 0-3 despite competing twice. In badminton, Brunei has yet to win a medal. [Jaspar Yu Woon Chai](/wiki/Jaspar_Yu_Woon_Chai "Jaspar Yu Woon Chai"), who was selected to represent Brunei in the men's singles competition at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), was the country's first badminton Olympian.

In [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), the Brunei Squash Rackets Association (BSRA) is the sport's national governing organisation, and are tasked to monitor the growth of squash across the nation. The winner of the men's open division of the Sarawak Squash Circuit was Sufri Abdul Hamid. He defeated fellow countryman Huzairie Abang Ali in the Squash Academy final at Pei Lin Middle School in [Miri](/wiki/Miri "Miri"). Saiful Rizal Mohd Ali triumphed over Hasnan Rozanan in a victory on 12 May 2018 at the squash centre of the [Hassanal Bolkiah National Sports Complex](/w/index.php?title=Hassanal_Bolkiah_National_Sports_Complex&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hassanal Bolkiah National Sports Complex (page does not exist)") to win the men's individual open championship of the BSRA Squash Tournament 2018. Huzairie Ali and Safwan Kifrawi, a squash team from Brunei, won [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") at the 6th Southeast Asia Cup Squash Championships 2020 in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). They rose to prominence in the domestic arena and created history by becoming the first team from their nation to win a medal at the Men's Jumbo Doubles competition at the SEA Cup.

### Precision sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Precision sports")]

In the 2015 Brunei International Skill-at-Arms Meet (BISAM) [shooting competition](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports"), Brunei was represented by 114 [marksmen](/wiki/Marksman "Marksman"). Two teams, representing Brunei Darussalam 'A' and 'B', will be made up of a total of 82 personnel of the [Royal Brunei Armed Forces](/wiki/Royal_Brunei_Armed_Forces "Royal Brunei Armed Forces") (RBAF), while Brunei Darussalam 'C' will be represented by 32 members of the [Royal Brunei Police Force](/wiki/Royal_Brunei_Police_Force "Royal Brunei Police Force") (RBPF). Notably, the yearly shooting match between various Women Police branches; while the branches competed for the year's Women Police Commandant Challenge Trophy, competitors in the event put their best marksmanship and gun handling skills to the test.

The only Olympian from Brunei to compete in two [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") is the [skeet shooter](/wiki/Skeet_shooting "Skeet shooting") [Prince Abdul Hakeem](/wiki/Prince_Abdul_Hakeem_Jefri_Bolkiah "Prince Abdul Hakeem Jefri Bolkiah"). In 2000, he finished 45th out of 49 competitors in the men's skeet shooting, which was the best result among Bruneian Olympians.

### Cycle sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cycle sports")]

See also: [Cycle sport](/wiki/Cycle_sport "Cycle sport")

All types of [cycle riding](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle") in Brunei are encouraged by the Brunei Darussalam Cycling Federation (BDCF). Via their collaboration with the [ASEAN Cycling Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Cycling_Confederation "Asian Cycling Confederation"), the BDCF are a wholly [non-profit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"), [volunteer](/wiki/Volunteering "Volunteering")-run organisation connected with the [Union Cycliste Internationale](/wiki/Union_Cycliste_Internationale "Union Cycliste Internationale") (UCI). Their mission is to elevate local cycle riding experiences, and reflect the whole diversity of cycling. The national cycling team's performance at the 2nd Mountain Bike (MTB) [Kerala International Mountain Cycling Competition](/w/index.php?title=Kerala_International_Mountain_Cycling_Competition&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kerala International Mountain Cycling Competition (page does not exist)") in [India](/wiki/India "India") was bittersweet. Nurjamri Johari was the top cyclist for Brunei, placing sixth with a time of 1:57:30 in the International Cross Country Competition XCO (Elite Men) category, where only 16 of the 26 starters completed the event. For the [2017 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2017_SEA_Games "2017 SEA Games") (29th SEA Games) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), the national cycling team hoped to provide a serious challenge.

### Water sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Water sports")]

See also: [List of Bruneian records in swimming](/wiki/List_of_Bruneian_records_in_swimming "List of Bruneian records in swimming") and [List of World Aquatics member federations](/wiki/List_of_World_Aquatics_member_federations "List of World Aquatics member federations")

The [Brunei Amateur Swimming Association](/w/index.php?title=Brunei_Amateur_Swimming_Association&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brunei Amateur Swimming Association (page does not exist)") (BASA) oversee [swimming](/wiki/Swimming "Swimming") competitions nationally and internationally.

At the [2013 World Aquatics Championships](/wiki/2013_World_Aquatics_Championships "2013 World Aquatics Championships"), [Muhammad Isa Ahmad](/wiki/Muhammad_Isa_Ahmad "Muhammad Isa Ahmad") competed in the [breaststroke](/wiki/Breaststroke "Breaststroke") events from the [2012 FINA World Swimming Championships](/wiki/2012_FINA_World_Swimming_Championships_(25_m) "2012 FINA World Swimming Championships (25 m)"), where he now holds the national record with a time of 31.29 seconds. Isa participated in the men's 50 metre breaststroke and men's 100 metre breaststroke events for Brunei at the [2019 World Aquatics Championships](/wiki/2019_World_Aquatics_Championships "2019 World Aquatics Championships") in [Gwangju](/wiki/Gwangju "Gwangju"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"). Isa made his Olympic debut for Brunei at the [Tokyo 2020 Games](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") by swimming the 100 metre breaststroke in 1:08.65.

[Anderson Lim Chee Wei](/wiki/Anderson_Lim "Anderson Lim") competed in the 200 metre freestyle preliminary round at the [Aquatics Centre](/wiki/London_Aquatics_Centre "London Aquatics Centre") of [Olympic Park](/wiki/Olympic_Park,_London "Olympic Park, London") at the [London 2012](/wiki/London_2012 "London 2012") Olympic Games in England. In his event, the Brunei's first Olympian swimmer easily broke the previous national record with a time of 2:02.26. On 16 July 2022, [Zeke Chan](/wiki/Zeke_Chan "Zeke Chan"), beat Anderson Lim's ten-year-old national record in the 200 metre freestyle event with a timing of 2:00.46.

[Christian Nikles](/wiki/Christian_Nikles "Christian Nikles") participated in the 50 and 100 metre [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_swimming "Freestyle swimming") events at the [2011 World Aquatics Championships](/wiki/2011_World_Aquatics_Championships "2011 World Aquatics Championships"), but he was unable to make it past the quarter-finals. In the 2019 World Aquatics Championships, he competed in the men's 50 and 100 metre freestyle events representing Brunei. He failed to qualify for the semi-finals in either of the events.

National swimmer Joel Ling Thai Yu won silver in the men's 200 metre [backstroke](/wiki/Backstroke "Backstroke") event at the [2022 Sukma Games](/wiki/2022_Sukma_Games "2022 Sukma Games") with a timing of 2:12.91, beating his previous record of 2:14:68 established in the preliminary round. Joel also beat his personal record of 1:00.91 set at the 29th Brunei Age Group Championship 2022 by clocking a new national record in the men's 100 metre backstroke event.

[Hayley Wong](/wiki/Hayley_Wong "Hayley Wong") broke both the age group and national records for girls between the ages of 14 and 15 in the [19th FINA World Championships](/wiki/2022_World_Aquatics_Championships "2022 World Aquatics Championships") in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") in 2022. Hayley set a new national record in the 200 metre [butterfly](/wiki/Butterfly "Butterfly") event with a timing of 2:35.48, breaking [Maria Grace Koh](/wiki/Maria_Grace_Koh "Maria Grace Koh")'s previous mark. She broke a record that had existed since 21 June 2008, when her predecessor finished in a time of 2:39.86.

### Endurance sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Endurance sports")]

At the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") in Rio de Janeiro, [Maizurah Abdul Rahim](/wiki/Maizurah_Abdul_Rahim "Maizurah Abdul Rahim") participated in the [women's 200 metre event](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_200_metres "Athletics at the 2016 Summer Olympics – Women's 200 metres"). At the end of the Games, she carried the Brunei flag during the closing ceremony.

The first female participant from Brunei ever to compete in an [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") occurred at [London 2012](/wiki/London_2012 "London 2012") when [Maziah Mahusin](/wiki/Maziah_Mahusin "Maziah Mahusin") broke the national athletics record in the women's 400 metres. Seven years later, she won silver and gold for 100 and 200 metre [hurdles](/wiki/Hurdling "Hurdling") during the [2019 Sarawak Open Track and Field Championship](/w/index.php?title=2019_Sarawak_Open_Track_and_Field_Championship&action=edit&redlink=1 "2019 Sarawak Open Track and Field Championship (page does not exist)").

Brunei [sprinter](/wiki/Sprint_(running) "Sprint (running)") [Muhd Noor Firdaus Ar-Rasyid](/wiki/Muhd_Noor_Firdaus_Ar-Rasyid "Muhd Noor Firdaus Ar-Rasyid") ran a time of 21.99 seconds in the 200 metres at the [2017](/wiki/2017_World_Championships_in_Athletics "2017 World Championships in Athletics") and [2019 World Athletics Championships](/wiki/2019_World_Athletics_Championships "2019 World Athletics Championships"). With a time of 21.39 seconds, he presently holds the record for the men's 200 metre competition at the [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games").

### Combat sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Combat sports")]

[Silat Melayu](/wiki/Silat_Melayu "Silat Melayu") is widely practised in Brunei, like its neighbouring countries in the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago"). The local martial art in Brunei is called 'Silat Suffian Bela Diri'.

At the 19th World Pencak Silat Championships in [Melaka](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), two bronze medals were won by Brunei. The men's and women's regu events in the competition from July 26 to 31 yielded both medals. In the men's division, the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") were defeated in the quarter-finals by the team of Ali Saifullah Abdullah, Hazim Ramliee, and Haziq Aqwa. They lost to [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") in the semi-finals, earning a joint bronze medal. The team of [Anisah Najihah](/wiki/Anisah_Najihah "Anisah Najihah"), [Nur Wasiqah Aziemah](/wiki/Nur_Wasiqah_Aziemah "Nur Wasiqah Aziemah"), and Norleyermah Raya, on the other hand, defeated [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") in the round of eight, but lost to [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") in the semi-finals.

During the [18th Sukma Games](/wiki/2016_Sukma_Games "2016 Sukma Games") at the Stadium Perpaduan in [Petra Jaya](/wiki/Petra_Jaya "Petra Jaya"), [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching"), siblings Hyde Nachman Hijium and Hyde Nathania Hijium represented Brunei by winning the bronze medals in the [poomsae](/wiki/Poomsae "Poomsae") male individual and poomsae female individual events, respectively. Hyde Nachman of Brunei Darussalam defeated Mic Daniel Siaw of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") is competing for the male individual gold medal, and Hyde Nathania defeated Maitheli A/P Mohana Sundaram of [Pulau Pinang](/wiki/Penang "Penang") to continue competing for the female individual gold medal.

## Team sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Team sports")]

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Football")]

Main article: [Football in Brunei](/wiki/Football_in_Brunei "Football in Brunei")

The most popular team sport in Brunei is [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). The [Brunei national football team](/wiki/Brunei_national_football_team "Brunei national football team") joined [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1969, but has not achieved much success. Brunei's highest-level domestic football league is the [Brunei Super League](/wiki/Brunei_Super_League "Brunei Super League"), which is managed by the [Football Association of Brunei Darussalam](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Brunei_Darussalam "Football Association of Brunei Darussalam") (FABD). Its first football league was the [Brunei Premier League](/wiki/Brunei_Premier_League "Brunei Premier League"), which existed from 2002 until 2019.

### Sepaktakraw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sepaktakraw")]

At the [2017 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2017_SEA_Games "2017 SEA Games") (29th SEA Games), which were held at the Titiwangsa Stadium on 28 August 2017, the national [sepak takraw](/wiki/Sepak_takraw "Sepak takraw") team of Brunei took the bronze medal in the Men's Regu event. Despite finishing the [round robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") stage in fourth place and suffering straight-set losses to Malaysia, [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), and Philippines, the national team shared the bronze medal with Laos. The team's bronze medal marked the nation's 14th overall finish in the SEA Games that year.

### Rugby union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Rugby union")]

Main article: [Rugby union in Brunei](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Brunei "Rugby union in Brunei")

The [Brunei national rugby union team](/wiki/Brunei_national_rugby_union_team "Brunei national rugby union team") is in the 6th division.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Polo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Polo")]

The Brunei national [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo") team achieved bronze in the [2017 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2017_SEA_Games "2017 SEA Games") (29th SEA Games).

### Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Basketball")]

Brunei had a short-lived basketball tournament, the [Brunei Cup](/wiki/Brunei_Cup "Brunei Cup"), as well as a short-lived professional basketball team named [Brunei Barracudas](/wiki/Brunei_Barracudas "Brunei Barracudas"), which featured in the [ASEAN Basketball League](/wiki/ASEAN_Basketball_League "ASEAN Basketball League") from 2009 to 2012.

### Netball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Netball")]

Main article: [Netball in Brunei](/wiki/Netball_in_Brunei "Netball in Brunei")

The [Brunei national netball team](/wiki/Brunei_national_netball_team "Brunei national netball team") represents Brunei in international events, and is known by the nickname Royal Bees. The [INF World Rankings](/wiki/World_Netball_Rankings "World Netball Rankings") list Brunei at number 34 as of 2 December 2022. The Brunei national team won the bronze medal at the [2015 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2015_Southeast_Asian_Games "2015 Southeast Asian Games") as one of the two semi-finalists. For placing as one of the semi-finalists at the 2017 Southeast Asian Games, Brunei repeated this accomplishment. Brunei was led by [Sri Lankan](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") coach [Thilaka Jinadasa](/wiki/Tilaka_Jinadasa "Tilaka Jinadasa") during the 2017 Games. The [2019 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/2019_Southeast_Asian_Games "2019 Southeast Asian Games") were held in [Laguna](/wiki/Laguna_(province) "Laguna (province)"), [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), and the Brunei ladies again managed to win bronze.

## Major sport events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Major sport events")]

Main articles: [Brunei at the Olympics](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Olympics "Brunei at the Olympics"), [Brunei at the Paralympics](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Paralympics "Brunei at the Paralympics"), and [Brunei at the Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Commonwealth_Games "Brunei at the Commonwealth Games")

### Brunei Darussalam National Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Brunei Darussalam National Games")]

Brunei Darussalam holds its biennial national multi-sport event; the Brunei Darussalam National Games ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sukan Kebangsaan Brunei Darussalam*, [Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_alphabet "Jawi alphabet"): سوكن كبڠسأن بروني دارالسلام, abbreviated as SKBD) since 2012, involving young athletes representing the nation's [four districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Brunei "Districts of Brunei"), [government agencies](/wiki/Government_of_Brunei "Government of Brunei"), and [private organisations](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Brunei "List of companies of Brunei").

### International multi-sport events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: International multi-sport events")]

Brunei Darussalam participates in the neighbouring [Sukma Games](/wiki/Sukma_Games "Sukma Games") in Malaysia since 2000 as an invitational team; and regional international sporting events such as the [BIMP-EAGA Friendship Games](/wiki/BIMP-EAGA_Friendship_Games "BIMP-EAGA Friendship Games"), the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games"), and the [Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Games "Southeast Asian Games"). Global international multi-sport events partaken by Brunei include the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"). Brunei hosted the [1999 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/1999_Southeast_Asian_Games "1999 Southeast Asian Games") and the 2008 and 2018 BIMP-EAGA Friendship Games.

### Hassanal Bolkiah Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Culture_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Hassanal Bolkiah Trophy")]

Main article: [Hassanal Bolkiah Trophy](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah_Trophy "Hassanal Bolkiah Trophy")

[Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia")'s youth football competition is known as the Hassanal Bolkiah Trophy for ASEAN Youth Football Championship. Football players from ASEAN members under the age of 21 were allowed to take part in this. The ASEAN Football Federation and the National Football Association of Brunei Darussalam jointly organise the competition. The competition made its debut in 2002, and is always hosted by Brunei Darussalam.
